title: Bangers and Mash
subtitle: Pork sausages on buttery mashed potato with a dark onion gravy
excerpt: Bangers and mash is three separate things that come together on the plate: properly browned sausages, mash that is smooth and rich without being stiff, and a gravy built from the onion and the pan. The gravy is the element most often neglected and the element that makes the most difference.
sourceNotes: British pub cooking tradition. Sausages and mashed potato have been a pairing in British cookery since the 18th century. The dish acquired its colloquial name during World War II, when sausage skins were filled with water-heavy mixtures that burst when cooked.

glossaryTerms.definition: To add liquid to a hot pan after frying in order to loosen the browned deposits from the base and dissolve them into a sauce. Those deposits are concentrated flavour; scraping them into the liquid is how a simple pan sauce develops depth.

content.text: Bangers and mash is a dish where every component can be done well or done carelessly. The sausages need time in a hot oven to colour all the way round, not just on the bottom. The mash should be smooth and yielding, not stiff or claggy. The onion gravy, built in the same pan as the sausages, is what holds the whole plate together and should not be an afterthought.

items.prepNote: peeled and cut into even chunks

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C. Place the {{sausages-pork}} sausages in a roasting tin with a drizzle of oil and roast for 25 to 30 minutes, turning once halfway, until deeply browned all over. Remove from the tin and rest while you make the gravy.

While the sausages roast, cook the {{potato}} of potato in well-salted boiling water for 18 to 20 minutes until completely tender. Drain well and leave to steam-dry in the colander for 2 minutes. Return to the pan over a low heat and mash thoroughly. Beat in the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{whole-milk}} of warm milk. Season with salt and white pepper. Keep warm.
Melt the butter for the gravy in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add the {{onion}} sliced onion and cook for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until soft and golden.
Stir in the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and cook for 1 minute. Gradually add the {{stock-beef}} of beef stock, scraping up the residue to
the pan. Add the Worcestershire sauce. Simmer for 5 to 8 minutes until the gravy coats the back of a spoon. Season well.

items.cause: Over-mashed, breaking down the starch into a sticky paste.
items.fix: Mash until just smooth, no further. If using a ricer, pass once and fold in the butter and milk by hand. Electric mixers always over-work mash.

items.cause: Flour was added in one go rather than stirred in gradually.
items.fix: Add the stock in three additions, whisking smooth each time before adding more. If lumps form, strain the gravy through a sieve.

items.cause: Oven too hot, or they were pricked before cooking.
items.fix: Cook sausages at 200°C without pricking. Pricking allows the fat and moisture to escape; the sausage dries out and then bursts. The skin is meant to hold the filling under pressure.

content.text: Bangers and mash is one of the most durable dishes in British cooking, sustained by pub menus and home kitchens with equal loyalty. The name was fixed in the Second World War, when meat rationing forced a reduction in pork content and sausages became mostly water, which burst with a bang in the pan. The dish predates the name by a century. It is not complicated, but it requires attention: sausages that are merely pale, mash that is lumpy, and gravy from a packet are not bangers and mash.
